result.markdown:
---
title: "Project Proposal"
subtitle: "Anatomy of an Embargo Breach"
author: "Chen ZeXin, Meng Tingyu, Meng Xinyu"
date: "last-modified"
toc: true
number-sections: true
---

# Motivation

A merger between TenantThread and CivicLoom Realty Partners was meant to stay
secret until 6:00 PM on 5 June 2046. One hour before that, the details started
showing up on the public platform FleX, posted by accounts belonging to
TenantThread's own team of AI communication agents. The embargo broke. Now
CivicLoom's legal team wants to know one thing. Did the agents leak the merger on
purpose, or did the system simply fall apart under pressure?

The trouble is that the answer is not sitting in any one message. It is scattered
across two weeks of agent conversations, private notes, channel choices, and a
market sliding toward panic. A written report can hand a reader a conclusion, but
it cannot let that reader test it. They cannot follow a single agent through the
day, replay a particular hour, or check whether an early warning was really there
to be seen. Our goal is to take all that buried evidence and turn it into
something an investigator can explore directly, even if they have never written a
line of code.

# What Makes This Different

Most analyses of an incident like this stop at what happened, who posted what and
when. The data here holds something richer. Every agent records its private
reasoning at each step, split into how it reacted, how it deliberated, and how it
rationalised. That field is rarely used, and it is where the real story sits.

Our application is built around that idea. Alongside the usual view of actions
and channels, it lets a user watch how the agents talked themselves into the
breach, hour by hour, and it treats the team as a network so the user can see who
truly sat at the centre of the decisions. Pairing the private reasoning with a
network read of influence is the part we think is new, and it is what lets the
tool speak to the real question of intent against breakdown rather than just
charting the timeline.

# Problem Statement and Objectives

The raw data is a single JSON file. It holds 23 rounds, 912 messages, and 7
agents, and most of what matters is locked inside nested fields like the agents'
private reasoning. Reading it by hand is not realistic, and a fixed set of charts
forces everyone to accept one analyst's framing of the story. So we propose an
interactive Shiny application that lets a user investigate the breach on their
own terms.

With the application, a user will be able to do five things. They can rebuild the
sequence of events that led to the leak and see which decisions, and which parts
of the system, let the post slip through. They can map the team as a network and
find out which agents were central to how decisions flowed. They can hold each
agent's normal behaviour up against its behaviour on the crisis day, so what was
truly strange stands out from what was merely busy. They can hunt for the leading
indicators, the earlier moments when someone drifted off pattern, and work out
why none of them set off an alarm in time. And they can read the agents' own
private reasoning as it changed through the day, which is the clearest way to
judge whether this looks like intent or like people talking themselves into
something.

# The Data

The dataset is `MC1_final.json` from VAST Challenge 2026 Mini-Challenge 1. It
contains 23 rounds. The first 13 are spaced one business day apart and cover the
run up from 17 May to 4 June. The last 10 are hourly and cover the crisis itself
on 5 June.

Each round carries three blocks. The environment context gives the state of the
world that hour, including a market snapshot with the stock price and sentiment.
The communications block is the heart of it, holding all 912 messages. Every
message records who sent it, which channel it went through, what type it was, a
pointer to the message it replied to, the content itself, and for many of them an
internal state field that captures what the agent was privately thinking. That
private field is split into reacting, rationalizing, and deliberating, and it is
one of the richest parts of the dataset. The participants block lists who was
present in each round.

In network terms the communications are link records, since each message ties a
sender to a recipient and those ties build the reply network we analyse later.

Seven agents take part. There is a Legal agent, a Platform-Trust agent, a PR
agent, a Social-Manager agent, two interns, and a Judge agent that acts as the
automated compliance monitor. Six channels appear. Some are monitored, like the
team room and one to one chats. One is a private side huddle. Three are public,
namely official posts, personal posts, and anonymous posts. The gap between the
monitored channels and the unmonitored ones turns out to matter a great deal.

There is one data quality issue we have to handle with care. The stock price
field is reliable before the crisis but becomes contaminated on 5 June. It starts
carrying junk values, including literal NaN entries and at least one number that
is actually the company's revenue rather than its share price. Because of that,
we use market sentiment as our backbone signal for pressure over time, since it
is recorded cleanly in every single round. We will not pretend the broken prices
form a smooth line.

# Methodology and Analytical Approach

Our plan moves from raw JSON to a working application in three stages.

First comes data preparation. We read the nested JSON without letting R simplify
it automatically, so we keep control over the shape of the tables. Then we
flatten it into tidy tables. One row per message, one row per round of world
state, and a table of reply links built from the responding to field. This shared
backbone feeds every part of the app.

Next is the analysis. We combine three kinds of work. Behavioural analysis looks
at channel use, posting patterns, and message volume. Network analysis treats the
replies as a graph and measures each agent's role in it. Text analysis of the
private reasoning fields tracks how the agents' thinking shifts as the day goes
on. For the network we calculate several centrality measures, since each one
answers a different question. Degree shows who is most active and most exposed.
Betweenness finds the brokers who sit on the path between others, which points
straight at who was central to the leak. Eigenvector shows who is tied to other
influential agents rather than just well connected by count. Closeness shows who
could reach the rest of the team fastest when things moved hour by hour.

Last is the visualisation and the interaction. Every view we build becomes
something the user can control in the app. They can pick an agent, move through
time, switch between centrality measures or chart types, and watch the result
respond. The whole point is to let people explore, not to hand them a fixed set
of answers.

# Prototype and Storyboard

The application is split into five modules. Each one pairs a main view with
controls the user can play with, and together they fit into a single system. The
notes below describe what we intend to build. Hand drawn storyboard sketches of
each layout will be added here before the proposal is finalised.

## Module 1, the sequence of the breach

A timeline built on top of market stress across the two weeks, with a colour
coded chain of key moments laid over it. Those moments include the early
deviations from normal practice, the near miss, the day the Judge was installed,
the points where its guardrails were bent, and the breach itself.

For interaction, a user can hover any moment to read the full story of that hour.
They can toggle a view that separates what happened outside the company, meaning
the press, from what happened inside among the agents, and step through the
rounds with a time control.

## Module 2, the network of decisions

A reply network of the seven agents, drawn from who responded to whom. Node size
and colour carry the centrality measures, so the agents who brokered the flow of
decisions stand out from those on the edges. This module is where the user sees
that the breach came from the core of the team rather than the fringe.

For interaction, a user can switch between centrality measures such as degree,
betweenness, and eigenvector, choose a layout, pick a time window to see how the
network changed between the calm weeks and the crisis day, and click an agent to
highlight its connections.

## Module 3, normal behaviour against the breach

A dumbbell chart of each agent's public posting, baseline next to crisis day. It
makes the strangest part visible at a glance, which is how the roles flipped. The
agents who never posted in public suddenly took over the channels, while the PR
agent, whose whole job is public messaging, went quiet. A second view shows the
mix of channels shifting away from the open, monitored ones toward private and
anonymous posting.

For interaction, a user can filter by agent or by day, switch between raw counts
and share of messages, and pick a channel to see who used it and when.

## Module 4, the leading indicators

A view of the earlier warning signs set against the calm baseline, with the 29
May near miss called out. Next to it sits a searchable, filterable table of every
off pattern action across the two weeks, so a user can dig through the evidence
themselves rather than taking our word for it.

For interaction, a user can search and filter that log by agent, channel, or
keyword, click an event to see what surrounded it, and slide between the early
warnings and the crisis day.

## Module 5, the reasoning behind it

A view of how the agents reasoned in private over time, showing the drift from
calm deliberation, to raw reaction, to self justification. Alongside it we show
how the actual words changed. This is the module that speaks most directly to the
big question of intent against breakdown.

For interaction, a user can pick an agent and read its private reasoning across
the whole timeline, switch between the reasoning modes, and highlight the hours
where the justifying language first appears.

# R Packages

| Package | What we use it for |
|---|---|
| tidyverse | Cleaning and reshaping the data |
| jsonlite | Reading the nested JSON |
| lubridate | Working with the timestamps |
| tidygraph, ggraph | Building and drawing the reply network |
| igraph | Calculating network centrality measures |
| ggiraph, plotly | Interactive charts with hover and click |
| DT | Interactive, searchable tables |
| tidytext | Analysing the private reasoning text |
| ggrepel, patchwork, scales | Labelling, composing, and formatting figures |
| shiny, shinyWidgets | Building the interactive application itself |

# Project Schedule

The Gantt chart below lays out our timeline from the proposal through to final
submission. The main dates we are working toward are the proposal on 10 June, the
bulk of the app build through late June, the poster on 1 July, the presentation
on 4 July, and the final submission on 5 July.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code  code-summary="Gantt chart code (vistime)"}
pacman::p_load(vistime, ggplot2)
schedule <- read.csv(text = "event,group,start,end
,Proposal,2026-06-06,2026-06-10
,Data preparation,2026-06-08,2026-06-16
,Shiny app development,2026-06-12,2026-06-30
,Project website,2026-06-08,2026-07-04
,Poster,2026-06-24,2026-07-01
,User guide,2026-06-26,2026-07-04
,Final submission,2026-07-04,2026-07-05")
gg_vistime(schedule, title = "Project Timeline")
```
:::
